frames.caption: Start at n = 19. Repeatedly replace n with the sum of squares of its digits, recording every value in a HashSet. Reach 1 → happy; revisit a value → cycle → unhappy.
frames.note: The digit-square-sum is a deterministic function f, so the sequence n, f(n), f(f(n)), ... is fully determined and must eventually repeat — there are only finitely many values it can pass through. Recording each n in a HashSet turns "have I been here before?" into an O(1) membership test. Either the walk hits 1 (happy) or it revisits a value it has seen, which proves a non-1 cycle (unhappy).

frames.caption: n = 19 ≠ 1, and seen.Add(19) returns true (new). Record 19, then compute SquareDigitSum(19).
frames.note: The digit-square-sum is a deterministic function f, so the sequence n, f(n), f(f(n)), ... is fully determined and must eventually repeat — there are only finitely many values it can pass through. Recording each n in a HashSet turns "have I been here before?" into an O(1) membership test. Either the walk hits 1 (happy) or it revisits a value it has seen, which proves a non-1 cycle (unhappy).

frames.caption: Digit 9: sum += 9² = 81 → sum = 81; drop it (n /= 10 → 1).
frames.note: SquareDigitSum peels digits right to left with d = n % 10 (last digit) and n /= 10 (drop it), accumulating d*d. Each digit is 0..9 so d*d <= 81 and the running sum stays tiny — no overflow risk. This f is the single transition that drives the whole walk; the only question is whether iterating it lands on 1 or loops.

frames.caption: Digit 1: sum += 1² = 1 → sum = 82; drop it (n /= 10 → 0).
frames.note: SquareDigitSum peels digits right to left with d = n % 10 (last digit) and n /= 10 (drop it), accumulating d*d. Each digit is 0..9 so d*d <= 81 and the running sum stays tiny — no overflow risk. This f is the single transition that drives the whole walk; the only question is whether iterating it lands on 1 or loops.

frames.caption: SquareDigitSum returns 82: digits 9, 1 → 9² + 1² = 82. Set n = 82.
frames.note: SquareDigitSum peels digits right to left with d = n % 10 (last digit) and n /= 10 (drop it), accumulating d*d. Each digit is 0..9 so d*d <= 81 and the running sum stays tiny — no overflow risk. frames.caption: Digit 0: sum += 0² = 0 → sum = 0; drop it (n /= 10 → 10).

frames.caption: Digit 0: sum += 0² = 0 → sum = 0; drop it (n /= 10 → 1).

frames.caption: SquareDigitSum returns 1: digits 0, 0, 1 → 0² + 0² + 1² = 1. Set n = 1.
frames.note: SquareDigitSum peels digits right to left with d = n % 10 (last digit) and n /= 10 (drop it), accumulating d*d. Each digit is 0..9 so d*d <= 81 and the running sum stays tiny — no overflow risk. This f is the single transition that drives the whole walk; the only question is whether iterating it lands on 1 or loops.

frames.caption: n is now 1, so the while condition n != 1 is false and the loop stops. Reaching 1 means the number is happy → return true.
frames.note: Hitting 1 ends the walk: f(1) = 1 is a fixed point, the happy attractor. Because the set never reported a repeat before 1 appeared, the path 19 → 82 → 68 → 100 → 1 funneled straight into it. Same answer with Floyd's pointers, which would meet at 1 itself for a happy number — the set version just trades O(k) memory for simpler code.
